content.text:
Many applications require approximations to the second derivative or 
higher, and often we need first-derivative approximations more accurate 
than $\bigo{h^2}$. In this section, we develop systematic methods for 
constructing finite difference formulas of arbitrary order.

The most important higher derivative formula approximates $f''(x)$ 
using three symmetrically placed points.

content.title: Three-Point Second Derivative Formula
content.content:
For $f \in C^4[a,b]$ and $x \pm h \in [a,b]$, there exists 
$\xi \in (x-h, x+h)$ such that
\[
\frac{f(x+h) - 2f(x) + f(x-h)}{h^2} = f''(x) + \frac{h^2}{12}f^{(4)}(\xi).
\]
The truncation error is $\bigo{h^2}$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Using Taylor expansions to fourth order:
$$
f(x+h) &= f(x) + hf'(x) + \frac{h^2}{2}f''(x) + \frac{h^3}{6}f'''(x) 
+ \frac{h^4}{24}f^{(4)}(\xi_1), \\
f(x-h) &= f(x) - hf'(x) + \frac{h^2}{2}f''(x) - \frac{h^3}{6}f'''(x) 
+ \frac{h^4}{24}f^{(4)}(\xi_2).
$$

Adding these equations:
\[
f(x+h) + f(x-h) = 2f(x) + h^2 f''(x) + \frac{h^4}{24}[f^{(4)}(\xi_1) + f^{(4)}(\xi_2)].
\]

By the Intermediate Value Theorem, there exists $\xi \in (x-h, x+h)$ with 
$f^{(4)}(\xi) = \frac{1}{2}[f^{(4)}(\xi_1) + f^{(4)}(\xi_2)]$. Thus:
\[
\frac{f(x+h) - 2f(x) + f(x-h)}{h^2} = f''(x) + \frac{h^2}{12}f^{(4)}(\xi). \qedhere
\]

Notice that the formula for $f''(x)$ divides by $h^2$. When $h$ is small, 
this amplifies round-off errors even more severely than for $f'(x)$.

content.title: Third Derivative Formulas
content.content:
For $f \in C^5$, the central difference approximation to $f'''(x)$ is:
\[
f'''(x) \approx \frac{f(x+2h) - 2f(x+h) + 2f(x-h) - f(x-2h)}{2h^3}
\]
with truncation error $\bigo{h^2}$.

Using only forward differences:
\[
f'''(x) \approx \frac{f(x+3h) - 3f(x+2h) + 3f(x+h) - f(x)}{h^3}
\]
with truncation error $\bigo{h}$.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Fourth Derivative Formula
content.content:
For $f \in C^6$, the central difference approximation to $f^{(4)}(x)$ is:
\[
f^{(4)}(x) \approx \frac{f(x+2h) - 4f(x+h) + 6f(x) - 4f(x-h) + f(x-2h)}{h^4}
\]
with truncation error $\bigo{h^2}$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
We apply the second derivative formula recursively. Let 
$g(x) = f''(x)$. Then
\[
f^{(4)}(x) = g''(x) \approx \frac{g(x+h) - 2g(x) + g(x-h)}{h^2}.
\]

Substituting the approximation $g(y) \approx \frac{f(y+h) - 2f(y) + f(y-h)}{h^2}$:
$$
f^{(4)}(x) &\approx \frac{1}{h^2} \cdot \frac{1}{h^2}\Big[
[f(x+2h) - 2f(x+h) + f(x)] \\
&\quad - 2[f(x+h) - 2f(x) + f(x-h)] \\
&\quad + [f(x) - 2f(x-h) + f(x-2h)]\Big] \\
&= \frac{f(x+2h) - 4f(x+h) + 6f(x) - 4f(x-h) + f(x-2h)}{h^4}. \qedhere
$$

content.text:
Using three points, we can achieve second-order accuracy even with 
one-sided formulas, which is essential near boundaries.

content.title: Three-Point Forward Formula
content.content:
For $f \in C^3$:
\[
f'(x_0) = \frac{-3f(x_0) + 4f(x_0+h) - f(x_0+2h)}{2h} + \frac{h^2}{3}f'''(\xi)
\]
for some $\xi \in (x_0, x_0+2h)$. The truncation error is $\bigo{h^2}$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Let $P(x)$ be the quadratic polynomial interpolating $f$ at 
$x_0$, $x_0+h$, $x_0+2h$. By Lagrange interpolation:
\[
P(x) = f(x_0) \frac{(x-x_0-h)(x-x_0-2h)}{(-h)(-2h)} 
+ f(x_0+h) \frac{(x-x_0)(x-x_0-2h)}{h(-h)}
+ f(x_0+2h) \frac{(x-x_0)(x-x_0-h)}{2h \cdot h}.
\]

Differentiating and evaluating at $x = x_0$:
$$
P'(x_0) &= f(x_0) \frac{-h + (-2h)}{2h^2} 
+ f(x_0+h) \frac{-2h}{-h^2}
+ f(x_0+2h) \frac{-h}{2h^2} \\
&= f(x_0) \cdot \frac{-3}{2h} + f(x_0+h) \cdot \frac{2}{h} 
+ f(x_0+2h) \cdot \frac{-1}{2h} \\
&= \frac{-3f(x_0) + 4f(x_0+h) - f(x_0+2h)}{2h}.
$$

The error follows from the interpolation error formula.

content.title: Three-Point Backward Formula
content.content:
For $f \in C^3$:
\[
f'(x_0) = \frac{f(x_0-2h) - 4f(x_0-h) + 3f(x_0)}{2h} + \frac{h^2}{3}f'''(\eta)
\]
for some $\eta \in (x_0-2h, x_0)$. The truncation error is $\bigo{h^2}$.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Boundary Derivative Approximation
content.solution:
Using the three-point forward formula:
\[
f'(0) \approx \frac{-3(1) + 4(1.1052) - 1.2214}{0.2} 
= \frac{-3 + 4.4208 - 1.2214}{0.2} = \frac{0.1994}{0.2} = 0.997.
\]

The exact value is $f'(0) = 1$, so the error is approximately $0.003$.

Compare with the two-point forward difference:
\[
f'(0) \approx \frac{1.1052 - 1}{0.1} = 1.052,
\]
which has error $0.052$—about 17 times larger.

content.text: Using five points, we can achieve fourth-order accuracy.

content.title: Five-Point Central First Derivative
content.content:
For $f \in C^5$:
\[
f'(x) = \frac{f(x-2h) - 8f(x-h) + 8f(x+h) - f(x+2h)}{12h} + \frac{h^4}{30}f^{(5)}(\xi)
\]
for some $\xi \in (x-2h, x+2h)$. The truncation error is $\bigo{h^4}$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
We seek coefficients $a$, $b$, $c$, $d$, $e$ such that
\[
f'(x) \approx \frac{1}{h}[af(x-2h) + bf(x-h) + cf(x) + df(x+h) + ef(x+2h)]
\]
is exact for polynomials up to degree 4. Expanding each $f(x+kh)$ in 
Taylor series and matching coefficients of $f(x)$, $f'(x)$, $f''(x)$, 
$f'''(x)$, $f^{(4)}(x)$ yields the system:
$$
a + b + c + d + e &= 0 \quad (\text{coeff of } f(x)) \\
-2a - b + d + 2e &= 1 \quad (\text{coeff of } hf'(x)) \\
4a + b + d + 4e &= 0 \quad (\text{coeff of } h^2 f''(x)) \\
-8a - b + d + 8e &= 0 \quad (\text{coeff of } h^3 f'''(x)) \\
16a + b + d + 16e &= 0 \quad (\text{coeff of } h^4 f^{(4)}(x))
$$

By symmetry, $a = -e$ and $b = -d$, and $c = 0$. Solving:
$a = 1/12$, $b = -8/12$, $d = 8/12$, $e = -1/12$.

content.title: Five-Point Central Second Derivative
content.content:
For $f \in C^6$:
\[
f''(x) = \frac{-f(x-2h) + 16f(x-h) - 30f(x) + 16f(x+h) - f(x+2h)}{12h^2} 
+ \frac{h^4}{90}f^{(6)}(\xi).
\]
The truncation error is $\bigo{h^4}$.
content.label: Theorem

content.text: The derivation of the five-point formula illustrates a general technique.

content.title: Method of Undetermined Coefficients
content.content:
To find a formula of the form
\[
f^{(m)}(x) \approx \frac{1}{h^m}\sum_{k=-p}^{q} c_k f(x + kh),
\]
we expand each $f(x+kh)$ in Taylor series, substitute, and require 
the resulting expression to match $f^{(m)}(x)$ through terms of 
desired order.

content.title: Order Conditions
content.content:
A formula using $n$ points can achieve at most order $n-1$ accuracy 
for the first derivative. Specifically, to achieve order $p$, the 
coefficients must satisfy:
\[
\sum_{k} c_k k^j = 
\begin{cases}
0 & j = 0, 1, \ldots, m-1, m+1, \ldots, p+m-1 \\
m! & j = m
\end{cases}
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Deriving a Four-Point Formula
content.problem:
Find a formula for $f'(x_0)$ using $f(x_0)$, $f(x_0+h)$, $f(x_0+2h)$, 
$f(x_0+3h)$ that achieves maximum accuracy.

content.title: Choosing the Right Formula
content.content:
More points do not always mean better results! Higher-order formulas:

- Require more function evaluations

- Are more sensitive to noisy data

- May not be applicable near boundaries

- Have larger round-off error constants
The three-point central difference is often the best practical choice.
